title: Editing Inside *insert mode*
subtitle: {key:Ctrl-W}, {key:Ctrl-U}, {key:Ctrl-H} — readline survivors.
summary: *insert mode* has its own tiny set of editing shortcuts inherited from readline: Ctrl-W (kill word), Ctrl-U (kill to start), Ctrl-H (backspace), Ctrl-T/Ctrl-D (indent/outdent).

blocks.text: *insert mode* has a small built-in vocabulary of editing shortcuts inherited from old Unix line editors. They're worth memorizing — they're faster than {key:Ctrl-O} for tiny corrections.

rows: Delete to start of line (or start of insertion)

blocks.title: Heritage Note
blocks.text:
{key:Ctrl-W}, {key:Ctrl-U}, {key:Ctrl-H} are *line discipline* characters from Unix terminals (the kernel's `termios` would erase a word/line/char before the program ever saw the input). Vim provides them inside *insert mode* for muscle-memory continuity.

The deletions respect `'backspace'` setting — by default, modern Vim allows backspacing past where you started inserting, past the start of the line, even past indent. With `set backspace=` (empty), {key:Ctrl-U} would only delete back to the start of the *current* insertion.
{key:Ctrl-W} for typo-on-last-word is faster than {key:Esc}{key:b}{key:c}{key:w} or {key:Ctrl-O}{key:d}{key:b}.
